text:
Image restoration (IR) has been widely recognized as an
important problem in computer vision and image process-
ing since decades ago [10, 13, 14, 18, 19, 26, 27, 29, 30, 32].
It focuses on recovering original images from degraded or
corrupted versions caused by noise, blur, or compression ar-
tifacts. The state-of-the-art methods are based on deep neu-
ral network, which combines convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) [15, 19] for local feature extraction and transform-
ers [18, 33] for modeling long-range dependencies within
images. Recently, state space models (SSMs) [9, 10] have
been proposed to model long-range dependencies with lin-
ear scaled computation relative to the sequence length.
However, the huge model size of SSM-based architecture
remains a challenge for real-time deployment on resource-
constrained devices. This calls for SSM compression ap-
proaches in the IR field, which remains largely underex-
plored.
Low-bit quantization is an efficient way to compress
Deep Learning (DL) models and accelerate model  infer-
ence [7, 17, 23]. Model memory footprint and latency can
be significantly reduced by converting weights and activa-
tions in floating-point precision to low-bit integer values (4
bits or fewer). However, quantizing SSMs presents a signifi-
cant challenge with severe quantization failures and perfor-
mance degradation in low-bit settings.
Firstly, structural outliers persistently exist in the system
(see Fig. 3), and their interaction with the inherent variabil-
ity of input data creates a fundamental challenge for normal
static quantization. Specifically, to accommodate outliers,
the quantization range must be expanded.  However,  this
expansion inevitably reduces the representation density in
the data-dense regions, where most values are concentrated.
Conversely, if the range is constrained to preserve density,
critical information from outliers may be truncated. Such
a trade-off originates from the static design of conventional
2

quantization ranges.  These ranges are predetermined and
remain fixed for all input instances, lacking the capacity to
adapt to dynamic data distributions. Secondly, prevalent de-
terministic rounding in low-bit quantization leads to back-
ward gradient mismatch and forward parameter homoge-
nization.  It leads to fundamental discretization errors in-
ducing nonlinear error propagation and even feature degra-
dation.  It forces suboptimal compromises between preci-
sion retention and high computational efficiency due to dis-
cretization. Both concurrent preservation of high-frequency
textures and sparse extreme-value activations are critical in
Super-Resolution tasks.
Inspired by  the above  insight,  we first  propose a
Dynamic-balancing Learnable Scalar (DLS). It is achieved
by designing a learnable scalar to dynamically adapt the
quantization range by capturing activation distribution char-
acteristics. We further propose a Range-floating Flexible
Allocator (RFA), which is a flexible discretization mecha-
nism with a flexible allocator using adaptive thresholds to
round values. RFA is proven effective in mitigating param-
eter homogenization through local gradient preservation,
thereby maintaining the feature representation capacity of
the SSM block.
Extensive  experiments  demonstrate  that  our  Q-
MambaIR outperforms  state-of-the-art  (SOTA)  quan-
tization methods  across  various  bit widths,  including
Post-training Quantization (PTQ) and Quantization-Aware
Training (QAT) methods (see Tabs. 1 and 3 to 5),  in the
context of image restoration (IR) models.  Notably, our
Q-MambaIR with 4 bits achieves performance comparable
to existing PTQ methods using 8 bits.  The experimental
results show that the proposed QAT methods significantly
improve model  accuracy over  the state-of-the-art  tech-
niques, even with aggressive low 2-bit weight quantized
QAT. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our Q-
MambaIR compared to both QAT- and PTQ-quantized
networks,  highlighting  its  superiority  over  different
methods.  Our main contributions are summarized as
follows:
• We propose Q-MambaIR, an accurate Mamba for effi-
cient image restoration. It narrows the gap between low-
bit quantized models and their full-precision counterparts,
enabling effective deployment  in resource-constrained
scenarios.
• To address extreme value preservation, we propose a
Dynamic-balancing Learnable Scalar (DLS). Specifically,
our SDLQ adjusts quantization ranges through a learnable
perceptron, effectively reducing truncation loss by captur-
ing statistical characteristics of activation distributions.
• To enhance high-frequency feature retention, we propose
a Range-floating Flexible Allocator (RFA), which pre-
serves critical details through adaptive threshold-based
discretization while ensuring robust feature extraction ca-

Mamba for Image restoration.  In recent years,  the in-
tegration of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) has
fundamentally transformed traditional approaches to image
restoration, substantially enhancing performance by lever-
aging the inherent advantages of neural networks [15, 19].

2. Related work
Mamba for Image restoration.  In recent years,  the in-
tegration of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) has
fundamentally transformed traditional approaches to image
restoration, substantially enhancing performance by lever-
aging the inherent advantages of neural networks [15, 19].
Subsequently, Transformer-based methods [18, 27, 33] have
further advanced the field by effectively modeling long-
range dependencies and capturing intricate relationships be-
tween spatially distant pixels. More recently, MambaIR
[10] has garnered significant attention within the research
community due to its innovative application of State Space
Model (SSM) linear recurrence properties, which enable ef-
ficient long-range dependency modeling. Nevertheless,  it
suffers from its substantial model size and intensive compu-
tational requirements, which significantly hinder its deploy-
ment for efficient image processing in resource-constrained
environments. In this context, low-bit quantization emerges
as an attractive strategy, offering the potential to harness the
benefits of SSM for image restoration tasks while maintain-
ing computational efficiency.
Quantization for IR/Mamba. Quantization methods can
3

be broadly classified into two categories based on the in-
volvement of training: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
and Post-Training Quantization (PTQ). QAT involves re-
training the neural network with quantized parameters, en-
abling the model to adapt to quantization-induced perturba-
tions and achieve an optimized solution with improved loss
[3, 6, 8].  In contrast, PTQ applies quantization directly to
a pre-trained model without additional training, providing
a more computationally efficient implementation, albeit po-
tentially at the cost of reduced accuracy.
The linear recurrence property of SSM introduces unique
challenges for quantization, particularly in activation quan-
tization. The existence of outliers is challenging for quanti-
zation.  Current PTQ approaches for SSMs primarily ad-
dress these challenges through rotation-based techniques.
For example, Quamba proposes a static 8-bit per-tensor
quantization method combined with the Hadamard trans-
form to smooth activations [5].  Similarly, MambaQuant
employs Karhunen-Loe`ve Transformation (KLT)-enhanced
rotation and Smooth-Fused rotation to alleviate activation
quantization issues [25]. However, these methods are cur-
rently limited to 8-bit quantization and have primarily been
applied in the context of language models.
QAT, with its training-based optimization, holds signifi-
cant potential for achieving ultra-low-bit quantization. For
instance, QuantSR [23] demonstrates the feasibility of ac-
curate and efficient super-resolution (SR) processing under
low-bit quantization [11, 16, 23].  Nevertheless, conven-
tional methods are constrained by their reliance on static
quantization ranges, which limits their effectiveness in han-
dling SSMs with outlier-prone activations. Consequently,
there is a critical need for further research to develop SSM-
tailored quantization, enabling robust and efficient ultra-
low-bit quantization in IR.
3. Method
To provide a solution for SSM low-bit quantization, We
first introduce an overview of the main part Visual State
Space Model (VSSM) employed in MambaIR followed by
an analysis of the limitations inherent in existing low-bit
quantization architectures.  Building on this analysis, we
propose our Q-MambaIR to address them,  an accurate
quantizedMamba for Efficient IR (Overview in Fig. 4). Our
Q-MambaIR mainly consists of two techniques: Dynamic-
balancing Learnable Scalar (DLS) and Range-floating
Flexible Allocator (RFA). DLS overcomes the constraints
of deterministic quantization ranges by dynamically adapt-
ing to the distribution of activations,  thereby minimizing
truncation loss caused by outlier clipping. RFA introduces
a soft rounding mechanism with a flexible rounding thresh-
old, which mitigates the accumulated errors typically asso-
ciated with static rounding schemes.

3.1. Preliminaries
Visual State Space Model. The VSSMmodule is the main
functioning feature extractor in MambaIR. The architecture
of VSSM is demonstrated in Fig. 5(a).  It adapts SSM to
vision data without compromising its advantages. Eq.

Quantization of VSSM. As a common practice in quan-
tization,  the weights and activations used in computation
units are mapped to a uniform discretized sequence by the
quantizer:
⌊clip ((X − β) · α)⌋
Q(x, α, β) =  + β  (4)

where Q(x, α, β)  represents  the quantized weights or
activations  in  low bit-width,  and X is  the  input  ten-
sor  in floating-point  format.  The function clip(x) =
n−1  n−1
max(min(x, 2  − 1), −2  ) ensures that values are
constrained within the representable range of n-bit integers
before rounding. α is the scaling factor, which determines
how the range of X is mapped to the rounding range be-
n−1  n−1
tween −2  and 2  − 1.  β is the shifting factor that
adjusts the zero-point alignment, affecting the distribution
of quantization range.
Limitation.  Existing low-bit quantization architectures
face significant limitations when applied to SSM for im-
age restoration tasks.  (1) While learnable parameters such
as alpha and beta enable per-operator dynamic adjustment
of quantization ranges, they still provide a fixed range for
each operator, lacking adaptability to varying input images.
This rigidity leads to substantial information loss, particu-
larly in the presence of outlier activations, which are com-
mon in SSM. (2) Conventional static rounding mechanisms.

In this paper, we propose Q-MambaIR, an accurate, effi-
cient, and flexible Quantized Mamaba for Image Restora-
tion tasks, addressing critical challenges in low-bit quan-
tization of SSM-based models.  The proposed network
employs a statistical Dynamic-balancing Learnable Scalar
(DLS) for dynamic quantization range and a Range-floating
Flexible Allocator (RFA) for detail gradient preservation.
we effectively mitigate the issues of outlier accommodation,
gradient mismatch, and parameter homogenization, while
preserving high-frequency textures. We demonstrate that Q-
MambaIR outperforms existing state-of-the-art quantized
IR networks in both accuracy and computational efficiency
with extensive experiments.  Learnable Scalar (DLS) for
dynamic quantization range and a Range-floating Flexible
Allocator (RFA) for detail gradient preservation. we effec-
tively mitigate the issues of outlier accommodation, gradi-
ent mismatch, and parameter homogenization, while pre-
serving high-frequency textures. We demonstrate that Q-
MambaIR outperforms existing state-of-the-art quantized
IR networks in both accuracy and computational efficiency
with extensive experiments.
